etymology: Niels Bohr (1885-1962) — the Danish physicist whose atomic model (1913) introduced quantum mechanics to atomic structure and whose complementarity principle and Copenhagen interpretation shaped 20th-century physics. Bohr received the 1922 Nobel Prize in Physics and directed the Institute for Theoretical Physics in Copenhagen, which also predicted hafnium's location in the periodic table.

timeline.event: 1981, GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research, Darmstadt, Germany (Münzenberg and Hofmann group)
Longest-lived known isotope: 270Bh, t½ = 61 seconds. Individual atoms detected by SHIP velocity filter.
IUPAC formally confirms the discovery and approves the name 'bohrium' and symbol 'Bo'. Element 107 officially joins the periodic table.

Bohrium was first produced at GSI by bombarding bismuth-209 with chromium-54 ions. A 2000 experiment at the Paul Scherrer Institute detected volatile BhO₃Cl molecules — confirming that bohrium forms oxychlorides analogous to rhenium, consistent with its position in Group 7. Named after the physicist whose atomic model explained chemical periodicity.

Research continues at JINR Dubna, GSI/FAIR, RIKEN, and other facilities. Element 107 represents the current frontier of nuclear synthesis — beyond it lies terra incognita, where nuclear stability predictions become increasingly uncertain and experimental access requires advances in accelerator technology and target production.

story: GSI discovered elements 107 through 112 in a remarkable sequence from 1981 to 1996, using bismuth and lead targets and transition metal beams. The SHIP separator — designed by Münzenberg and Hofmann — was the key instrument that enabled detection of single atoms of these superheavy elements.
source_note: Discovered by Münzenberg and Hofmann group (1981) at GSI Darmstadt. Named after Niels Bohr. Published in Zeitschrift für Physik A, 1981.
